name: Divine Marriage
shortDescription: Divine marriage, or hierogamy, in Japanese mythology refers to the sacred union of deities, often to create the world or populate it with other gods and beings.
description: Divine marriage, also known as hierogamy, is a central theme in many mythologies worldwide, and Japanese mythology is no exception. It represents the sacred union between deities, often symbolizing the cosmic harmony and creative forces necessary for the creation and sustenance of the universe. In the Japanese context, the most prominent example is the marriage of Izanagi and Izanami, who descended from Takamagahara, the heavenly realm, to create the islands of Japan. Their union, though ultimately tragic, is foundational to the Shinto creation myth. The concept of divine marriage extends beyond just procreation; it signifies the establishment of order from chaos, the balancing of opposing forces (yin and yang parallels can be drawn), and the legitimization of power structures. The offspring of divine marriages often inherit divine qualities, becoming rulers or deities themselves, reinforcing the divine right of kings and establishing lineages. The rituals surrounding marriage in Japanese culture, even today, can be seen as echoes of this ancient belief in the power and sacredness of union. The disruption of the divine marriage, as seen with Izanami's death, often leads to imbalance and chaos, highlighting the importance of maintaining harmony within the divine realm. These stories serve not only as explanations for the origins of the world but also as moral lessons about relationships, duty, and the consequences of disharmony.

sources.title: Kojiki
sources.author: Ō no Yasumaro (translator: Basil Hall Chamberlain)
